scenario: At Solara Water Utilities, the OT team wants to deploy an AI model directly onto the SCADA historian server to flag anomalous valve behavior in real time. A newly hired OT security lead pushes back, citing the December 2025 CISA/NSA/ASD joint guidance on secure AI integration in OT.
question: Per the December 2025 CISA/NSA/ASD joint guidance “Principles for the Secure Integration of Artificial Intelligence in Operational Technology,” which practice should the team prioritize before deploying the AI model?
options: Skip governance review since the model only flags anomalies and does not control equipment
Push OT data to a separate, secured AI system outside the safety-critical control loop, with a human in the loop for critical decisions
Embed the model directly into the PLC firmware for the fastest possible response time
Deploy the model without fail-safe mechanisms since anomaly detection is monitoring-only
aria_hint: The guidance's architecture principle is about where AI processing should physically happen relative to the safety-critical loop.
debrief: B is correct: the joint CISA/NSA/ASD guidance recommends keeping AI processing off the plant floor by sending sanitized OT data to a separate, secured AI system, paired with human-in-the-loop oversight for critical decisions. Option A skips governance entirely -- the guidance requires review regardless of whether the model controls equipment directly. Option C embeds the model into the safety-critical loop itself, exactly what the guidance warns against. Option D deploys without fail-safes, again contrary to the guidance's core recommendation.

sources.text: NSA/CISA/ASD joint guidance, "Principles for the Secure Integration of Artificial Intelligence in Operational Technology" (3 Dec 2025)
